q: Monty Hall: three doors, one car. You pick a door; the host, who knows where the car is, opens another door revealing a goat and offers a switch. Do you take it?
a: Switch — it wins ⅔ of the time. Your first pick was right only ⅓ of the time, and the host's reveal concentrates the other ⅔ on the remaining door.

q: 100 closed lockers. Person n toggles every n-th locker, for n = 1…100. Which lockers end up open?
a: The perfect squares: 1, 4, 9, …, 100 — ten lockers. A locker is toggled once per divisor, and only squares have an odd number of divisors.

q: Two ropes each burn in exactly 60 minutes, but unevenly. Measure 45 minutes.
a: Light rope A at both ends and rope B at one end. A dies at 30:00; at that instant light B's other end — its remaining 30 minutes halve to 15. Total: 45.

q: 25 horses, a 5-lane track, no stopwatch. Minimum races to find the fastest three?
a: Seven. Five heats, then a race of the heat winners, then one last race among the five horses that could still be 2nd or 3rd.

q: What's the expected number of rolls of a fair die to see all six faces at least once?
a: 6 × (1 + ½ + ⅓ + ¼ + ⅕ + ⅙) = 14.7 rolls — the coupon-collector problem.

q: 100 passengers board in order, but the first one lost his ticket and sits randomly. Everyone else takes their own seat if free, otherwise a random one. P(last passenger gets his own seat)?
a: Exactly ½. By symmetry the last seat contested is always seat #1 or seat #100, with equal probability.

q: Three ants sit on the corners of a triangle and each walks along a randomly chosen edge. Probability of no collision?
a: ¼. They avoid colliding only if all go clockwise or all counter-clockwise: 2 of the 8 equally likely combinations.

q: A lily pad patch doubles every day and covers the whole pond on day 48. When was the pond half covered?
a: Day 47. Exponential growth has no respect for intuition.

q: How many people in a room before the odds of a shared birthday pass 50%?
a: 23. With 23 people there are 253 pairs — more chances than the calendar can dodge.

q: Flipping a fair coin: which takes longer on average to appear — HH or HT?
a: HH takes 6 flips on average, HT only 4. After an H, a tails wastes nothing while waiting for HT, but resets you completely while waiting for HH.

q: A hallway has three switches; one controls a bulb in a closed room. You may flip switches freely but enter the room once. Which switch is it?
a: Flip #1 on for a few minutes, turn it off, flip #2 on, walk in. Lit → #2; dark and warm → #1; dark and cold → #3.

q: You're offered even-money bets on a coin that lands heads 60% of the time. What fraction of your bankroll should you bet each flip?
a: Kelly says f* = 2p − 1 = 20%. Bet more and your long-run growth rate falls; bet double-Kelly and it hits zero.

q: A casino pays 2ⁿ if the first head appears on toss n. The expected value is infinite — so why won't anyone pay $1,000 to play?
a: The St. Petersburg paradox: EV is infinite but utility isn't. Log utility (or any bankroll constraint) prices the game in single digits.

q: A test for a disease afflicting 1 in 10,000 is 99% sensitive and 99% specific. You test positive. P(sick)?
a: Under 1% (≈0.98%). The 1% false-positive rate swamps the 0.01% base rate — Bayes beats intuition.
